title: India
html:
India

India shows up across the corpus in three distinct registers: as a macro-prediction in 2020, as trip-prep in 2024, and as travel-story-bank in the same year. The arc is one of the cleanest examples in the vault of a Yahir thesis being lived out: he predicted China and India as the post-pandemic decade's center of gravity at age 17, then went and walked through them himself five years later.

The earliest mention is in 2020 03 30 facebook pandemic watchlist, the late-March 2020 brain dump where 17-year-old Yahir is sketching post-COVID futures: "India infrastructure" sits in a list with China tracking apps, Great Depression analogs, surveillance state from contact tracing, and mass digital migration. India is on his macro radar before it's on his itinerary.

By May 2024 (2024 05 13 things) it has become operational logistics: "India + Nepal backpack — visas, train system research, route plan, daily budget, travel-blog/IG/WordPress site, packing list" with the cleanest single-line confession of pre-trip uncertainty in the corpus — "wtf are we gonna do in Nepal?" The trip is bridge-positioned: it sits between the OSU finals push and the Shanghai study abroad visa scramble. India is the warm-up for China.

The actual lived India is in 2024 08 14 travel occurrences, the August 2024 catalog Yahir keeps banking memorable mishaps in. Three India entries: "Being assaulted by an elderly Indian man in Varanasi", "Checking into the Nagpur police station with Yoav", and "Bringing Yoav to an Indian hospital — saw a roaming monkey and a near-dead man." These are the three most cinematic Yahir-on-the-road moments in the entire pre-China travel arc, and they cluster geographically — Varanasi (north), Nagpur (central), with Yoav as the recurring travel partner. The hospital scene with the roaming monkey is the most-quoted detail; it's the kind of image he banks for the future "could be a great travel content creator" line in 2024 11 28 leading thoughts about 6 month adventure.

The India entries in the travel-occurrences catalog are also telling for what they're not: no temples named, no food called out, no "best meal of my life" entry. The India in Yahir's notes is the adversarial India — the one that shows up as plot. The reverent / scenic version probably exists in long-form travel writing he hasn't ingested into the wiki yet.
